8  The Pattern Stack (Levels of Patterns)

title: 9  Pattern as Graph: The Computable Representation
text:
9.1 Summary
Patterns are represented as typed structural graphs—the representation that makes patterns computable and enables automated analysis.

9.1.1 Must-Remember: Key Concepts








Concept
Definition
Why It Matters




Pattern Graph
Nodes (entities from System Ontology) + Edges (relationships) + Constraints (conditions)
Aligns patterns with the same primitives used for blueprint descriptions


Pattern Signature
The minimal set of nodes, edges, and constraints that uniquely identifies a pattern
Enables subgraph matching—detecting patterns within larger blueprint graphs


Pattern Variant
A modification of the base pattern via optional/alternative components or relations
Captures real-world diversity without fragmenting the catalog


Pattern Instance
A concrete occurrence of a pattern within a specific system
Grounds the catalog in empirical architectural practice


Pattern Evidence
Observable signals (dependencies, config files, infra manifests) that support pattern detection
Provides traceability and transparency for automated analysis





9.1.2 Core Principles

Pattern detection = subgraph matching: the blueprint graph is searched for structural configurations matching a pattern’s signature.
Signatures are minimal, not exhaustive: they capture only the anchors needed to recognize the pattern. Strong and weak indicators are distinguished to support probabilistic detection.
Patterns support variants through OR/XOR slots: this accommodates different implementation choices while preserving a stable core identity.
Evidence + Confidence → probabilistic detection: pattern presence is not binary; confidence values weighted by evidence strength enable ranking and human review.
Graph representation bridges narrative and computation: the same pattern can be understood by humans (narrative) and analyzed by machines (graph).
crumbs: Part I - Pattern Theory From Narrative to Computable
9  Pattern as Graph: The Computable Representation

title: 10  Pattern Catalog Object Model
text:
10.2 Summary
The Pattern Catalog Object Model transforms patterns from informal descriptions into standardized, structured catalog entries with well-defined fields.

10.2.1 Must-Remember: Catalog Entry Fields








Field
Purpose
Key Question It Answers




Pattern Identity
ID, Name, Type, Level
What is this pattern called and how is it classified?


Problem Statement
The architectural challenge addressed
Why does this pattern exist?


Context & Preconditions
Environmental conditions and assumptions
When is this pattern applicable?


Forces & Constraints
Competing pressures and technical limitations
What trade-offs shape this structure?


Structure
Components, interfaces, data, workflows
How is the pattern built?


Consequences
Benefits and trade-offs
What price do I pay for adopting it?


Implementation Notes
Optional practical guidance
How do I implement it in practice?


Anti-Patterns
Common failure modes
What should I avoid?


Ontology Hooks
Mapping to System Ontology entities and relations
How does this connect to the formal ontology?





10.2.2 Core Principles

The object model is the descriptive layer—it helps humans understand what a pattern does, when it applies, and what trade-offs it introduces.
Structure is the operational core: the structural description (components, interfaces, data, workflows) is what enables mapping to blueprint elements.
Anti-patterns are first-class citizens: documenting failure modes is as important as documenting the correct structure.
Ontology Hooks bridge narrative and computation: they align pattern definitions with the formal ontology, enabling pattern detection and blueprint integration.
Object Model + Signature = two-layer representation: the object model (Chapter 4) provides descriptive knowledge; the signature (Chapter 5) provides computational anchors. Both are necessary.

title: 11  Pattern Signature Specification
text:
11.1 Summary
A Pattern Signature is the computable structural fingerprint of a pattern—the minimal set of anchors required to recognize it within a system.

11.1.1 Must-Remember: The Five Anchor Types








Anchor Type
What It Identifies
Example




Entity Anchors
Required component types
Message broker, worker pool, plugin registry


Relation Anchors
Interactions connecting components
Invocation, message publication, subscription


Interface Anchors
Contractual boundaries (APIs, schemas)
Extension point interface, event schema


Data Anchors
Ownership/lifecycle boundaries of data
Which components produce, consume, or own data


Constraint Anchors
Operational conditions required
Reliability guarantees, concurrency rules, isolation





11.1.2 Must-Remember: Signature Mechanics







Concept
Definition




Strong indicator
An anchor whose presence strongly suggests the pattern (e.g., a centralized orchestrator → workflow engine)


Weak indicator
An element that may appear in many systems, providing supporting (not decisive) evidence


OR slot
One of several alternative elements may appear


XOR slot
Mutually exclusive alternatives—exactly one must appear


Confidence value
Estimated likelihood that the pattern is present, derived from weighted evidence


Evidence model
Traceable records of the observable signals supporting each anchor





11.1.3 Core Principles

Signatures enable three operations: pattern retrieval (by structural features), pattern clustering (by structural similarity), and pattern extraction (by matching against blueprints).
Strong vs. weak indicators enable probabilistic detection: not all anchors are equal; weighting improves detection reliability.
Variant slots (OR/XOR) allow flexibility: patterns can accommodate real-world variation while maintaining a stable identity.
Evidence + Confidence keeps detection auditable: every pattern inference can be traced back to its supporting signals.
Signatures are the bridge to computation: without signatures, patterns are just narratives; with signatures, they become searchable, matchable, analyzable knowledge objects.

title: 12  Pattern Tagging & Classification System
text:
12.1 Summary
Tags provide the navigational metadata layer that complements the structural identity defined by pattern signatures. Together they form a dual-access system: precision via signatures, exploration via tags.

12.1.1 Must-Remember: Tag Categories








Tag Category
What It Describes
Example Values




Domain
Application domains where the pattern commonly appears
Financial systems, AI platforms, data processing, developer tooling


System-Type
Type of system architecture where the pattern participates
Web services, CLI tools, data pipelines, agent systems, platforms


Quality Attribute
System properties the pattern primarily influences
Scalability, reliability, security, extensibility, performance


Complexity
Operational maturity required to adopt the pattern
Low (simple mechanism) → High (substantial infrastructure)


Compatibility
How the pattern interacts with other patterns
Depends on, conflicts with, frequently paired with





12.1.2 Core Principles

Signatures answer what a pattern is; tags answer where and why it is used.
Tags support exploratory discovery: browsing, filtering by domain, and finding patterns for specific quality attributes.
Compatibility tags prepare for pattern composition: they identify dependencies and potential conflicts that become critical when combining patterns.
The dual system (signatures + tags) serves both humans and machines: systematic analysis through structural matching; exploratory navigation through metadata filtering.
Part II framework is complete: Object Model (what patterns are) + Signature (how patterns are identified) + Tags (how patterns are organized) = the formal representation foundation.

title: 13  Layered System Pattern
text:
13.2 Summary
The Layered System pattern organizes functionality into conceptual layers with controlled, unidirectional dependencies flowing downward.

13.2.1 Must-Remember: The Four Canonical Layers








Layer
Responsibility
Dependencies




Presentation
Handle external interaction (HTTP, CLI, UI)
Depends on Service layer


Service
Coordinate application-level operations, orchestrate domain logic
Depends on Domain layer


Domain
Core business logic, entities, rules, invariants
Depends on Storage layer


Storage
Persistence and data retrieval (repositories, DB adapters)
No upward dependencies





13.2.2 Must-Remember: Key Rules







Rule
Why It Matters




Dependencies flow downward
Changes in upper layers don’t affect lower layers; the domain and storage layers remain stable


Each layer has a distinct responsibility
Mixing concerns (e.g., putting business logic in the presentation layer) destroys modularity


Adjacent layers communicate through controlled interfaces
Skip-layer dependencies or shortcuts lead to tight coupling





13.2.3 Common Degenerations to Avoid

Anemic Domain Model: the domain layer becomes a data container; business logic migrates to services, creating bloat.
Broken dependency direction: shortcuts bypassing layer boundaries erode the separation and produce a tangled dependency graph.


The layered pattern’s value is not complexity—it is the discipline of separation. When layers are cleanly separated, systems become easier to understand, maintain, and evolve.
crumbs: Part III - Foundational Patterns (The Core Library)

title: 14  Modular Monolith Pattern
text:
14.1 Summary
The Modular Monolith pattern organizes a system as a single deployable unit with strong internal module boundaries. It proves that modularity and distribution are separate concerns.

14.1.1 Must-Remember: Key Concepts







Concept
Description




Module
A cohesive functional area with encapsulated internal logic and explicit interfaces


Internal API
Contract through which modules interact; no direct access to internal details


Shared infrastructure
Cross-cutting services (logging, config, persistence) shared across modules


Single deployment boundary
The entire system ships as one artifact, unlike microservices





14.1.2 Must-Remember: Why It Matters

Modularity ≠ Distribution: you can achieve strong separation of concerns without incurring the operational overhead of distributed services.
Evolutionary path to microservices: modules with explicit interfaces can be extracted into independent services when scale or team structure justifies it.
Avoids false binary: the real choice is not “monolith vs. microservices” but “structured vs. unstructured monolith.”
Bridge architecture: serves as the intermediate stage between early-stage monolithic systems and fully distributed architectures.


The modular monolith is the architecture most teams should default to. It provides the benefits of modularity without the costs of distribution—until those costs are justified.
crumbs: Part III - Foundational Patterns (The Core Library)

title: 15  Microservices Pattern
text:
15.1 Summary
The Microservices pattern decomposes a system into independently deployable services, each aligned with a specific business capability.

15.1.1 Must-Remember: Core Design Decisions







Decision
Guidance




Service boundaries
Align with bounded contexts (Domain-Driven Design); each service owns a coherent business capability


Communication
Synchronous (HTTP/RPC) for immediate responses; asynchronous (events/messaging) for decoupled, resilient interaction


Data ownership
Database per service — each service owns its persistence; others access data only through the service’s API


Infrastructure
API gateway (routing), service discovery (dynamic location), and messaging infrastructure are required additions





15.1.2 Must-Remember: When to Adopt







Adopt When…
Avoid When…




Teams need independent deployment cycles
System scale doesn’t justify operational complexity


Different services have different scaling requirements
Team lacks operational maturity (CI/CD, monitoring, incident response)


Organizational structure aligns with service boundaries (Conway’s Law)
Services would be tightly coupled, creating a “distributed monolith”





15.1.3 Common Pitfalls

Poorly defined boundaries → tight coupling across services, distributed monolith behavior.
Premature adoption → operational overhead without corresponding benefits.
Shared databases → breaks service autonomy, creates hidden coupling.


Microservices are an operational trade, not just an architectural one. The operational complexity they introduce must be actively managed through mature infrastructure and disciplined boundary design.
crumbs: Part III - Foundational Patterns (The Core Library)

title: 16  Event-Driven System Pattern
text:
16.1 Summary
The Event-Driven System pattern organizes system interaction around asynchronous event production and consumption, introducing temporal decoupling between components.

16.1.1 Must-Remember: Core Elements







Element
Role




Producer
Emits events describing state changes or significant occurrences


Broker
Mediates event distribution; routes events from producers to consumers via topics or channels


Consumer
Subscribes to topics and reacts to events independently


Event
A structured message describing something that happened; includes schema, metadata, and payload





16.1.2 Must-Remember: Delivery Semantics








Semantics
Meaning
Practical Approach




At-least-once
Event delivered 1+ times; consumers must tolerate duplicates
Most common choice; requires idempotent consumers


Exactly-once
Each event processed precisely once
Theoretically desirable but requires complex coordination; rarely achieved in practice





16.1.3 Must-Remember: Reliability Mechanisms







Mechanism
Purpose




Idempotency
Processing the same event multiple times produces the same result


Retry
Failed processing attempts are retried automatically


Ordering (partitioning)
Events within a partition are ordered; parallel processing across partitions


Dead-letter queue
Isolates events that repeatedly fail processing for inspection




Events decouple producers from consumers, but they also introduce new failure modes. Every event-driven system must proactively address idempotency, delivery semantics, and observability to behave predictably under real-world conditions.
crumbs: Part III - Foundational Patterns (The Core Library)

title: 17  Pipeline Pattern
text:
17.1 Summary
The Pipeline pattern structures a system as a sequence of processing stages where data flows progressively through incremental transformations.

17.1.1 Must-Remember: Core Structural Elements







Element
Function




Stage
Processing unit that receives input, transforms it, and produces output


Transform
The specific operation performed by a stage (filter, aggregate, enrich, validate)


Sink
Endpoint that consumes the final output (database, file system, downstream service)


Data flow edge
Directed connection representing the movement of data between stages





17.1.2 Must-Remember: Batch vs. Streaming








Aspect
Batch Pipeline
Streaming Pipeline




Data unit
Discrete collections (batches)
Continuous events/records


Execution
Scheduled jobs
Long-running, event-triggered


Use case
Periodic analytics, reporting, ETL
Real-time monitoring, event processing


State
Stateless within a batch
May maintain windowed state





17.1.3 Must-Remember: Data Contracts

Every stage expects a defined input schema and produces a defined output schema → these are data contracts.
Schema changes must be managed through versioning and backward-compatible evolution.
Without explicit data contracts, a change in one stage can silently break downstream stages.
Triggers initiate pipeline execution: job schedulers (batch) or incoming data events (streaming).


Pipelines are the universal abstraction for sequential data processing. Their power comes from decomposition: complex transformations are broken into independent, reusable stages connected by explicit data flow contracts.
crumbs: Part III - Foundational Patterns (The Core Library)

title: 18  Workflow Engine Pattern
text:
18.2 Summary
The Workflow Engine pattern separates workflow coordination from task execution, enabling reliable orchestration of long-running, distributed processes.

18.2.1 Must-Remember: Core Components







Component
Role




Orchestrator
Central coordinator that interprets workflow definitions, schedules tasks, manages dependencies


State Store
Persists workflow execution state (task status, progress, retry history); enables recovery after failures


Task Queue
Communication channel between orchestrator and workers


Workers
Execute actual task logic; report results back to orchestrator/state store





18.2.2 Must-Remember: Reliability Mechanisms







Mechanism
Why It Matters




Retries
Failed tasks are re-executed automatically with configurable policies (fixed delay, exponential backoff, max limits)


Persistent state
Workflow execution survives crashes; resumes from last known state, not from the beginning


Idempotent tasks
Tasks must tolerate repeated execution without producing inconsistent results


DAG representation
Workflows are modeled as directed acyclic graphs of tasks and dependencies; enables visualization and analysis





18.2.3 Key Insight

Separation of concerns: the orchestrator manages what should happen (process logic); workers manage how (task execution). This separation enables each to scale and evolve independently.
Observability is a built-in property: because workflows are explicitly defined as structured graphs, the engine can expose task tracing, status tracking, and DAG visualization—capabilities that are difficult to achieve when process logic is embedded in application code.


Workflow engines make process execution visible, recoverable, and analyzable. They are the right choice when “just running a job” is not enough—when you need to track, resume, and audit multi-step processes.
crumbs: Part IV - High-Leverage System Patterns (The "Blueprint Generators")

title: 19  Job Queue + Worker Pool Pattern
text:
19.1 Summary
The Job Queue + Worker Pool pattern decouples task submission from task execution via an asynchronous queue, enabling scalable, resilient background processing.

19.1.1 Must-Remember: Core Elements







Element
Function




Queue/Broker
Stores pending jobs; acts as a buffer between producers and workers


Worker Pool
Set of concurrent processes/services that retrieve and execute jobs


Acknowledgment
Worker signals completion; unacknowledged jobs are reassigned





19.1.2 Must-Remember: Workload Management







Mechanism
Purpose




Backpressure
Controls how quickly jobs enter the pipeline when workers are saturated


Concurrency control
Determines how many tasks run in parallel; workers can scale horizontally


Priority queues
Urgent tasks are processed ahead of lower-priority ones


Timeout policies
Stalled jobs (exceeding time limits) are retried or redirected





19.1.3 The Exactly-Once Reality

Exactly-once processing is extremely difficult in distributed systems—it requires complex coordination protocols.
Pragmatic approach: adopt at-least-once delivery + idempotent job handlers. This achieves correctness without the cost of distributed coordination.
Idempotency is not optional: when the same job may be executed multiple times (due to retries, reassignment, or failures), handlers must produce the same result each time.


The queue is the great decoupler. It absorbs load spikes, buffers failures, and enables producers and consumers to evolve independently. Design workers to be idempotent—this single practice eliminates an entire class of distributed system bugs.
crumbs: Part IV - High-Leverage System Patterns (The "Blueprint Generators")
19  Job Queue + Worker Pool Pattern

title: 20  Plugin System Pattern
text:
20.1 Summary
The Plugin System pattern enables extensibility through externally provided modules that integrate with a core host system via well-defined extension points.

20.1.1 Must-Remember: Core Concepts







Concept
Description




Extension Point
Interface/hook that defines how plugins integrate with the host system


Plugin Registry
Tracks available plugins (identifiers, supported extension points, versions)


Dynamic Loading
Plugins are discovered, loaded, and integrated at runtime or initialization


Plugin Lifecycle
Managed phases: initialization → activation → execution → deactivation





20.1.2 Must-Remember: Critical Concerns







Concern
Why It Matters




Sandboxing
Plugins (often from third parties) must be isolated to prevent destabilizing the host—via separate processes, containers, or permission models


Security boundaries
Restrict which system APIs plugins can access; without this, plugins introduce vulnerabilities


Version compatibility
Extension interfaces are contracts; when they change, previously developed plugins break


Contract governance
Versioned interfaces, compatibility rules, and dependency management keep the plugin ecosystem stable





20.1.3 Key Insight

Extension points define the plugin system’s power: too narrow and the system isn’t extensible; too broad and the host loses control. Getting extension boundaries right is the central architectural decision.
A plugin system is not just a technical structure—it’s an ecosystem enabler. The long-term value comes from the community of extensions built around the platform.


Plugin systems trade host control for extensibility. The art is defining extension points that are powerful enough to enable real customization while tight enough to protect the integrity of the core platform.
crumbs: Part IV - High-Leverage System Patterns (The "Blueprint Generators")

title: 21  Policy Engine Pattern
text:
21.1 Summary
The Policy Engine pattern centralizes rule definition and evaluation, separating policy logic from application code to enable consistent, auditable governance.

21.1.1 Must-Remember: Core Functions







Function
Description




Store policies
Maintain policy definitions in a structured repository (declarative languages or structured formats)


Evaluate rules
Process incoming queries against stored policies to compute decisions


Return decisions
Determine whether an action is allowed, denied, or subject to additional constraints





21.1.2 Must-Remember: Architecture Elements







Element
Role




Policy Store
Repository for policy definitions and rule sets


Policy Evaluator
Engine that interprets policies and computes decisions


Enforcement Points
Components (API gateways, service endpoints, authorization layers) that request decisions and enforce outcomes





21.1.3 Must-Remember: Authorization Models







Model
How It Works




RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
Permissions are associated with roles; users are assigned roles


ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)
Decisions based on user, resource, and environment attributes; more flexible but more complex





21.1.4 Key Insight

Policy as Code: policies are not hard-coded in application logic—they are stored, versioned, and evaluated centrally. This means policy changes can be deployed without redeploying services.
Centralization reduces duplication: without a policy engine, each service implements its own authorization logic → inconsistent, hard to audit.
Separation of concerns: policy definition (what the rules are), policy evaluation (computing decisions), and policy enforcement (acting on decisions) are separated into distinct layers.


A policy engine turns governance into a configurable, auditable system. When authorization rules, compliance constraints, or operational policies change, you update the policy—not the code.
crumbs: Part IV - High-Leverage System Patterns (The "Blueprint Generators")

title: 22  Multi-Tenant Platform Pattern
text:
22.1 Summary
The Multi-Tenant Platform pattern enables a single system to serve multiple independent tenants within shared infrastructure while enforcing strong isolation guarantees.

22.1.1 Must-Remember: Three Dimensions of Isolation








Dimension
What It Protects
Implementation Strategies




Data isolation
Tenant data separation
Tenant ID in shared DB → dedicated schemas → fully isolated storage (escalating security)


Compute isolation
Workload non-interference
Containerization, virtualization, resource quotas, scheduling policies


Network isolation
Communication privacy
Network segmentation, service identity, secure routing





22.1.2 Must-Remember: Governance Mechanisms







Mechanism
Purpose




Metering
Track compute, storage, API calls, and other resource usage per tenant


Billing/Chargeback
Convert usage into cost models for commercial or internal allocation


Quota policies
Limit resource consumption to prevent one tenant from starving others





22.1.3 Key Insight

The tenant model is foundational: every resource, identity, and permission must be associated with a tenant identifier. Without an explicit tenant model, isolation cannot be enforced.
Isolation is a spectrum, not binary: the right level depends on the security/compliance requirements. Shared-database-with-tenant-ID is cheaper but weaker; dedicated-per-tenant infrastructure is stronger but more expensive.
Multi-tenancy is the architectural pattern behind SaaS: it enables infrastructure cost efficiency at scale while maintaining the perception of a private system for each tenant.


Multi-tenancy is the architecture of efficiency without sacrificing separation. The challenge is choosing the right isolation level for each dimension based on trust, compliance, and cost requirements.
crumbs: Part IV - High-Leverage System Patterns (The "Blueprint Generators")

title: 23  Observability Pattern
text:
23.2 Summary
The Observability pattern introduces structured mechanisms for monitoring, diagnosing, and understanding system behavior through three complementary telemetry pillars.

23.2.1 Must-Remember: The Three Pillars of Observability








Pillar
What It Captures
Use Case




Logs
Discrete timestamped events (actions, warnings, errors)
Debugging, incident investigation, detailed context


Metrics
Numerical measurements (request rates, latency, memory, error counts)
Continuous monitoring, alerting, trend analysis


Traces
End-to-end request flow across distributed components
Identifying bottlenecks, failure points, dependency analysis





23.2.2 Must-Remember: Correlation Mechanism







Concept
How It Works




Correlation ID
Generated when a request enters the system; propagated across all service boundaries


Trace Context
Structured metadata (trace ID + span ID) describing parent-child relationships between operations


Span
A single unit of work within a trace; each service adds its own span



Without correlation, distributed requests become invisible across service boundaries—you see each component in isolation but cannot reconstruct the full execution path.


23.2.3 Key Insight

Observability ≠ Monitoring: monitoring checks known failure modes against predefined thresholds; observability lets you ask new questions about unknown failure modes by inspecting rich telemetry.
The three pillars are complementary: logs give detail, metrics give trends, traces give causality. A system is truly observable only when all three are correlated.
Instrumentation must be built in, not bolted on: retrofitting observability is significantly harder than designing it from the start.


You cannot operate what you cannot observe. In distributed systems, observability is not a feature—it is a fundamental operational requirement.
crumbs: Part V - Reliability & Trust Patterns (Cross-cutting Patterns)

title: 24  Resilience Pattern Set
text:
24.1 Summary
Resilience patterns are cross-cutting structural safeguards that enable systems to withstand and recover from failures. They do not eliminate failures—they limit their scope and maintain acceptable behavior under degraded conditions.

24.1.1 Must-Remember: The Four Resilience Mechanisms








Pattern
What It Does
When to Use




Retry + Backoff
Re-attempts failed operations with progressively increasing delays
Transient failures (network blips, momentary resource exhaustion)


Circuit Breaker
Halts requests to a failing dependency when errors exceed a threshold; tests recovery with probe requests
Preventing cascading failures across service dependencies


Bulkhead
Isolates resources (thread pools, connection pools, execution environments) so one subsystem’s failure doesn’t starve others
Multi-tenant systems, mixed workloads with different criticality


Graceful Degradation
Reduces functionality while preserving core capabilities when full operation isn’t possible
Any system where partial availability is better than total outage





24.1.2 Must-Remember: Key Rules

Retries must always use backoff: naive retries amplify failures by flooding struggling services. Exponential backoff gives the target time to recover.
Circuit breakers protect the whole system: the cost of a trip (temporarily degraded functionality) is far less than the cost of a cascading outage.
Bulkheads enforce resource fairness: without them, a single misbehaving subsystem can exhaust shared resources and bring down the entire system.
Graceful degradation requires explicit design: identify essential vs. non-essential capabilities before a failure occurs.


Assume failures will happen—design for them. Retry + backoff handles transients, circuit breakers prevent cascades, bulkheads contain damage, and graceful degradation preserves core functionality when things go wrong.
crumbs: Part V - Reliability & Trust Patterns (Cross-cutting Patterns)

title: 25  Security Boundary Pattern
text:
25.1 Summary
The Security Boundary pattern introduces explicit architectural structures that define and enforce trust boundaries, centralizing authentication, authorization, and sensitive resource management at well-defined boundary points.

25.1.1 Must-Remember: Core Mechanisms







Mechanism
What It Does




Authentication Gateway
Verifies the identity of requesting entities at the system boundary before they reach internal services


Authorization Gateway
Evaluates whether the authenticated identity is permitted to perform the requested action (RBAC/ABAC)


Secrets Management
Stores encrypted credentials centrally; components retrieve secrets dynamically through secure channels—never embedded in code


Data Classification
Categorizes data by sensitivity (public, internal, confidential, restricted) and applies protection accordingly





25.1.2 Must-Remember: Key Rules







Rule
Why It Matters




Centralize security at boundaries
Authentication and authorization at the gateway ensures internal services can trust pre-validated requests


Never embed secrets in code
Secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens) must be retrieved dynamically from a secure store; embedding them risks exposure through source code


Classify data by sensitivity
Not all data needs the same protection; classification guides encryption, access, and auditing policies


Encrypt at rest and in transit
Sensitive data must be protected both when stored and when moving between services





25.1.3 Key Insight

Security boundaries should be few and explicit: the more boundary points, the more surface area to defend. A well-designed architecture has a small number of well-enforced trust boundaries.
Defense in depth: while boundary enforcement is critical, internal services should not blindly trust requests—defense in depth means multiple layers of verification.
Secrets management is non-negotiable: leaked credentials are one of the most common causes of security breaches. A secrets management system is not optional at any scale.


Security is not a feature—it is an architectural property enforced at boundaries. Centralize authentication and authorization at gateways, manage secrets through dedicated infrastructure, and classify data to apply the right level of protection.
crumbs: Part V - Reliability & Trust Patterns (Cross-cutting Patterns)

title: 26  Pattern Compatibility and Conflict Types
text:
26.2 Summary
When multiple patterns are combined, their assumptions may conflict. Understanding these conflict categories is essential for building coherent composed architectures.

26.2.1 Must-Remember: The Four Conflict Types








Conflict Type
Root Cause
Example




Interface
Incompatible interaction models or communication protocols
Synchronous request-response pattern × asynchronous event pattern


Data
Incompatible assumptions about data ownership, consistency, or lifecycle
Microservices (database per service) × integration pattern (shared repository)


Workflow
Multiple patterns attempting to control execution flow
Centralized orchestration × decentralized choreography


Constraint
Incompatible operational requirements or system guarantees
Aggressive retry (resilience) × strict rate limits (security)





26.2.2 Must-Remember: Compatibility Metadata







Metadata Type
What It Declares




Compatible with
Patterns that work well together (e.g., workflow engine depends on job queue)


Requires
Supporting patterns that must be present


Conflicts with
Patterns that introduce structural tensions


Caution
Conditions where combination is possible but requires careful design





26.2.3 Key Insight

Pattern composition is the ultimate goal: individual patterns are building blocks; the real architecture emerges from their combination.
Compatibility metadata prepares for composition: it doesn’t resolve conflicts automatically but provides the structural information needed for composition systems to detect and manage them.
Resolution strategies exist for each conflict type: interface gateways for interface conflicts, data ownership models for data conflicts, hierarchical control for workflow conflicts, and explicit prioritization for constraint conflicts.
Part VI establishes readiness, not algorithms: the actual composition algorithms (merge, embed, orchestrate) are deferred to the System Composition volume.


Patterns are designed to be combined—but combination requires care. Every pattern pairing should be evaluated across all four conflict dimensions before committing to a composed architecture.
crumbs: Part VI - Pattern Composition Readiness (Pre-Composition Rules)
26  Pattern Compatibility and Conflict Types

title: 27  Pattern-to-Blueprint Mapping Rules
text:
27.1 Summary
Pattern-to-Blueprint mapping rules define how patterns populate the blueprint kernel—the bridge between reusable pattern definitions and concrete system architectures.

27.1.1 Must-Remember: Blueprint Kernel Slots and Pattern Contribution








Kernel Slot
What the Pattern Contributes
Example




Goal
Architectural intent that motivates the pattern
“Orchestrate distributed tasks reliably” (Workflow Engine)


Capabilities
System capabilities enabled by the pattern
“Asynchronous task processing” (Job Queue)


Components
Architectural elements the pattern introduces
Broker, orchestrator, worker pool, plugin registry


Interfaces
Interaction contracts between components
APIs, message schemas, extension points


Data
Information structures owned by the pattern
Event streams, workflow state, policy definitions


Workflows
Dynamic processes coordinating system behavior
Task graphs, stage chains, event propagation flows





27.1.2 Must-Remember: Architectural Views








View
Perspective
What Patterns Contribute




Product View
Capabilities delivered
Functional modules and services


Architecture View
Structural relationships
Components, interfaces, data flows


Operations View
Infrastructure and deployment
Observability, scaling policies


Security View
Trust and protection
Boundaries, authorization, secrets management





27.1.3 Key Insight

Pattern variants map to optional/alternative blueprint slots: a job queue may support different queue implementations; these choices are captured as configuration fields in the blueprint.
Spec View is a derived projection: requirements and acceptance criteria can be generated from pattern definitions but are secondary artifacts—the blueprint is the authoritative representation.
Each pattern fills multiple kernel slots: a single pattern contributes to several slots simultaneously, making it a “blueprint generator” rather than a single-element contributor.


Patterns are not just descriptions—they are generators of blueprint structure. When mapped correctly, patterns populate the entire kernel and project into all architectural views, enabling systematic, repeatable architecture design.
crumbs: Part VI - Pattern Composition Readiness (Pre-Composition Rules)

title: 28  How to Curate a Standard Library
text:
28.2 Summary
A pattern library must be curated with the same discipline as a software library or formal ontology. Without clear curation rules, the collection becomes inconsistent, redundant, and untrustworthy.

28.2.1 Must-Remember: Pattern Inclusion Criteria








Criterion
Meaning
Why Essential




Stability
Appears repeatedly across independent systems
A one-off structure is not a pattern


Generality
Applies across multiple domains or system types
Domain-specific structures don’t belong in a general library


Recognizability
Has a clear structural signature detectable in blueprints
If it can’t be identified, it can’t be a reliable knowledge object





28.2.2 Must-Remember: Library Governance







Practice
Purpose




Versioning
Patterns evolve (new variants, refined signatures); versioning ensures compatibility with earlier blueprints


Anti-patterns
Documented failure modes serve as negative samples—helping architects recognize problematic designs and training automated detection


Naming conventions
Stable, unambiguous identifiers ensure patterns are referenceable across all tools and artifacts


Quality review
Expert evaluation verifies inclusion criteria before a pattern enters the standard library





28.2.3 Key Insight

Patterns are living artifacts: the catalog is not a fixed document—Pattern Catalog v0.1 is the first version of an evolving knowledge base.
Anti-patterns are as valuable as patterns: recognizing how structures commonly fail is essential for both human architects and automated analysis systems.
Governance scales trust: as the library grows, consistent naming, classification, and review processes ensure it remains a reliable resource.


A pattern library is only as good as its curation. Stability, generality, and recognizability are the gatekeepers—every pattern must earn its place in the catalog through demonstrated cross-system recurrence.

title: 29  Mining Patterns from Repositories (Bridge to Extraction)
text:
29.1 Summary
Pattern mining establishes a systematic pipeline from real-world repository signals to validated pattern definitions, bridging the gap between architectural practice and formal pattern libraries.

29.1.1 Must-Remember: The Mining Pipeline








Stage
What Happens
Output




Signal detection
Analyze repositories for structural clues (dependencies, config, directory structure)
Pattern candidates


Blueprint extraction
Construct blueprint representations from repository artifacts
System blueprints


Clustering
Group similar blueprint instances to detect recurring structural forms
Candidate pattern families


Human-in-the-loop abstraction
Architects identify structural invariants and define problem/context/trade-offs
Pattern draft


Validation
Verify the pattern appears consistently across additional systems
Validated pattern


Catalog entry
Formalize as a standard library entry with schema, signature, and tags
Standard library entry





29.1.2 Must-Remember: Key Principles







Principle
Why It Matters




Patterns come from real systems, not theory
Every pattern in this catalog emerged from repeated observation of real architectures solving similar problems


Clustering over blueprints, not documents
Structural similarity is detected in blueprint graphs, not in narrative descriptions


Humans are essential
Automated analysis detects structural similarities, but only humans can determine whether a cluster represents a meaningful pattern with coherent intent and trade-offs


Pipeline must be grounded in evidence
Every pattern inference must be traceable back to observable repository signals




Patterns are discovered, not invented. The mining pipeline ensures the catalog remains empirically grounded—new patterns emerge from real architectural practice, validated across multiple systems before admission to the standard library.
